sources.title: Lessons from Building Claude Code: Agent Tool Design and Progressive Disclosure
sources.summary: Agent tool design should match the model's capabilities and be discovered through experimentation. Tools need to evolve as model capabilities improve - what helps early models may constrain advanced ones. Progressive disclosure allows adding functionality without increasing tool complexity
key_takeaways: Agent tool design should match the model's capabilities and be discovered through experimentation
Tools need to evolve as model capabilities improve - what helps early models may constrain advanced ones
Progressive disclosure allows adding functionality without increasing tool complexity
Letting agents build their own context is more effective than pre-providing it
Successful tools are ones the model understands and wants to use
[Action] Design tools that match your model's specific capabilities through observation
[Action] Regularly revisit and update tools as model capabilities improve
[Action] Use progressive disclosure to add functionality without tool proliferation
[Action] Give agents search tools to build their own context rather than pre-providing it
[Action] Test tools by observing if the model understands and uses them effectively
[Action] Consider subagents for specialized tasks instead of adding more tools
sources.raw_excerpt:
## Introduction: Constructing Agent Action Spaces
- Building agent harnesses requires careful construction of action spaces
- Claude uses Tool Calling with primitives like bash, skills, and code execution
- Tool design should match the agent's capabilities, similar to choosing tools for solving math problems
- Framework: give agents tools shaped to their abilities by observing and experimenting
## Improving Elicitation with AskUserQuestion Tool
- Goal was to improve Claude's ability to ask questions and reduce friction
- Attempt 1: Added questions to ExitPlanTool - caused confusion about conflicting plans
- Attempt 2: Modified markdown output format - unreliable, Claude would deviate from format
- Attempt 3: Created dedicated AskUserQuestion tool - successful, Claude understood and used it well
- Tool shows modal, blocks agent loop until user responds, enables structured output
## Evolution from Todos to Tasks
- Initially used TodoWrite tool to keep Claude on track with reminders every 5 turns
- As models improved, todo lists became limiting rather than helpful
- Opus 4.5 got better at subagents but couldn't coordinate on shared todo lists
- Replaced TodoWrite with Task Tool for better agent-to-agent communication
- Tasks include dependencies, updates across subagents, and can be modified/deleted
- Important to revisit tool assumptions as model capabilities increase
## Search Interface Design and Context Building
- Initially used RAG vector database - powerful but required setup and was fragile
- RAG gave Claude context instead of letting it find context itself
- Introduced Grep tool to let Claude search codebase and build its own context
- Pattern: smarter Claude becomes better at building context with right tools
- Agent Skills formalized progressive disclosure for incremental context discovery
- Claude evolved from unable to build context to nested search across file layers
## Progressive Disclosure - Claude Code Guide Agent
- Claude Code has ~20 tools, high bar for adding new ones
- Claude lacked knowledge about how to use Claude Code itself
- Instead of adding to system prompt (context rot), used progressive disclosure
- First tried giving Claude link to docs - worked but loaded too much context
- Built Claude Code Guide subagent with specialized instructions for doc searching
- Successfully added functionality without adding tools
## Conclusion: Art vs Science
- No rigid rules for tool building - it's an art as much as science
- Depends on model, agent goals, and operating environment
- Key advice: experiment often, read outputs, try new things
- Learn to 'see like an agent'

📌 Highlight: Grep工具

key_takeaways: It's possible to build and ship real software products entirely with AI agents, achieving 10x development speed
Success requires shifting from writing code to designing environments and feedback loops for agents
Agent legibility is crucial - everything must be accessible in-repository context
Strict architectural constraints and automated enforcement enable speed without decay
High-throughput development changes merge philosophy - corrections are cheaper than waiting
[Action] Structure repository knowledge as progressive disclosure rather than monolithic documentation
[Action] Implement strict architectural constraints with custom linters for agent environments
[Action] Make application state (UI, logs, metrics) directly accessible to AI agents
[Action] Build automated cleanup processes to prevent AI-generated technical debt accumulation
[Action] Design agent-to-agent review processes to reduce human bottlenecks
[Action] Encode human taste and principles as mechanical rules rather than manual guidelines
sources.raw_excerpt:
## The Zero-Code Experiment
- Built entire software product with 0 manually-written lines of code using Codex
- Product has daily users and external alpha testers
- Estimated 1/10th the development time compared to manual coding
- Generated ~1 million lines of code over 5 months
- Team of 3-7 engineers managing agents, averaging 3.5 PRs per engineer per day
## Redefining Engineering Roles
- Engineers focus on systems, scaffolding, and leverage rather than direct coding
- Primary job became enabling agents to do useful work
- Work depth-first: break down goals into building blocks for agents
- Humans interact primarily through prompts and review processes
- Agent-to-agent review processes with Ralph Wiggum Loop methodology
## Making Applications Agent-Legible
- Made UI, logs, and metrics directly readable by Codex
- Implemented per-worktree app instances for isolated testing
- Integrated Chrome DevTools Protocol for UI interaction
- Exposed observability via LogQL and PromQL queries
- Agents can work autonomously for 6+ hours on single tasks
## Repository Knowledge Management
- Abandoned monolithic AGENTS.md approach due to context crowding and rot
- Structured docs/ directory as system of record with progressive disclosure
- AGENTS.md serves as table of contents (~100 lines)
- Mechanical enforcement via linters and CI jobs
- Recurring doc-gardening agent maintains documentation freshness
## Architecture and Quality Enforcement
- Rigid architectural model with strict layer dependencies
- Custom linters enforce invariants and taste preferences
- Business domains follow fixed layer structure (Types → Config → Repo → Service → Runtime → UI)
- Constraints enable speed without architectural drift
- Error messages include remediation instructions for agents
## High-Throughput Development Philosophy
- Minimal blocking merge gates due to high agent throughput
- Short-lived pull requests with quick corrections over waiting
- Agents handle entire development lifecycle end-to-end
- Full autonomy from bug reproduction to merge completion
- Human escalation only when judgment required
## Entropy Management and Cleanup
- Agents replicate existing patterns, leading to drift over time
- Replaced manual Friday cleanup (20% time) with automated processes
- Golden principles encoded as mechanical rules
- Background Codex tasks perform continuous refactoring
- Garbage collection approach to technical debt management

📌 Highlight: 工程师专注于系统、脚手架和杠杆作用，而非直接编码

## Introduction to Production-Grade Agentic AI Architecture
- Modern agentic AI systems are built as well-defined architectural layers, not single services
- Each layer handles specific concerns: agent orchestration, memory management, security, scalability, fault handling
- Production-grade systems combine layers for reliability, observability, and safety under real-world workloads
## Critical Monitoring Aspects
- Agent behavior monitoring: reasoning accuracy, tool usage correctness, memory consistency, safety boundaries, context handling
- System reliability monitoring: latency, availability, throughput, cost efficiency, failure recovery, dependency health
- Both aspects essential for operating multi-agent systems reliably at scale
## Seven-Layer Architecture Overview
- Article promises to cover 7 core architectural layers
- Includes Service Layer, Middleware, Context Management
- Focus on building production-ready agentic systems

sources.raw_excerpt:
## Introduction and Background
- Author has 10 months of daily Claude Code usage experience
- Won Anthropic x Forum Ventures hackathon using Claude Code
- Built PMFProbe project for startup validation
## Skills and Commands System
- Skills operate like rules for specific workflows and scopes
- Commands are executable via slash commands (/refactor-clean, /tdd, /e2e)
- Skills stored in ~/.claude/skills, commands in ~/.claude/commands
- Can chain multiple commands together in single prompt
- Codemap updater skill helps Claude navigate codebase without burning context
## Hooks System
- Trigger-based automations for specific events
- Hook types: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, UserPromptSubmit, Stop, PreCompact, Notification
- Example: tmux reminder before long-running commands
- Use hookify plugin to create hooks conversationally instead of manual JSON
- Hooks constrained to tool calls and lifecycle events
## Subagents
- Processes that main Claude can delegate tasks to with limited scopes
- Can run in background or foreground, freeing up context
- Examples: planner.md, architect.md, tdd-guide.md, code-reviewer.md
- Can be sandboxed with specific tool permissions
- Work well with skills for autonomous task execution
## Rules and Memory
- Rules folder holds .md files with best practices Claude should always follow
- Two approaches: single CLAUDE.md or modular rules folder
- Examples: security.md, coding-style.md, testing.md, git-workflow.md
- Rules include: no emojis in codebase, no purple hues in frontend, always test before deployment
## MCPs (Model Context Protocol)
- Connect Claude to external services directly
- Prompt-driven wrapper around APIs for more flexibility
- Examples: Supabase MCP for SQL queries, Chrome MCP for browser control
- Critical: context window management - be selective with enabled MCPs
- Rule of thumb: 20-30 MCPs in config, keep under 10 enabled
## Plugins
- Package tools for easy installation instead of manual setup
- Can combine skills + MCP or bundle hooks/tools together
- LSP plugins provide real-time type checking and intelligent completions
- Install via plugin marketplace commands
- Same context window warning applies as with MCPs
## Tips and Tricks
- Keyboard shortcuts: Ctrl+U (delete line), ! (bash prefix), @ (search files), / (slash commands)
- Parallel workflows: /fork for non-overlapping tasks, git worktrees for overlapping
- tmux for long-running commands and log monitoring
- mgrep > grep for better search capabilities
- Useful commands: /rewind, /statusline, /checkpoints, /compact
## Editor Integration
- Author prefers Zed editor for Claude Code workflow
- Zed benefits: Agent Panel Integration, Rust performance, CMD+Shift+R command palette
- VSCode/Cursor also viable with terminal format or extension
- Split screen setup: terminal with Claude Code + editor
- Enable auto-save, git integration, and file watchers
## Author's Complete Setup
- 14 plugins installed but only 4-5 enabled at a time
- 14 MCPs configured but only 5-6 enabled per project
- Custom hooks for tmux reminders, formatting, TypeScript checks
- Structured rules and subagents for different concerns
- Custom status line showing user, directory, git branch, context %, model, time, todos

📌 Highlight: Chrome MCP for browser control

📌 Highlight: Processes that main Claude can delegate tasks to with limited scopes

key_takeaways: AI agents don't get smarter over time, but their surrounding context files become richer and more precise

sources.raw_excerpt:
## Introduction and Problem Statement
- 40-day journey building OpenClaw AI agents from scratch
- Initial agents were ineffective - filled content with emojis and hashtags
- Solution wasn't changing prompts or models, but building a file-based memory system
- 8 agents now running 24/7 with high-quality output
## Core Philosophy: File-Based Memory System
- Agents don't get smarter over time, but their surrounding files become richer
- No message queues, databases, or complex orchestration - just Markdown files
- File system itself serves as the integration layer
- Accumulated context is the competitive moat
## Three-Layer Architecture
- Identity Layer: SOUL.md, IDENTITY.md, USER.md - defines who the agent is
- Operation Layer: AGENTS.md, HEARTBEAT.md - defines how agents work and self-heal
- Knowledge Layer: MEMORY.md, daily logs, shared-context/ - what agents have learned
- Each layer solves a specific core problem
## Identity Layer Implementation
- SOUL.md defines agent personality and core principles
- IDENTITY.md serves as quick reference card
- USER.md contains personal details like timezone, dietary preferences
- Personal details create compound effects across all interactions
## Operation Layer Implementation
- AGENTS.md defines session startup process and memory management rules
- HEARTBEAT.md monitors system health and handles failures
- Agents have no memory between sessions - everything must be written to files
- Self-healing mechanisms prevent system failures
## Knowledge Layer Implementation
- Three-tier memory system: MEMORY.md (refined), daily logs (raw), shared-context (cross-agent)
- MEMORY.md contains distilled lessons and error patterns
- Daily logs are pruned to prevent context bloat
- Shared-context enables cross-agent knowledge sharing
## Agent Collaboration Through Files
- No API calls or message queues - collaboration happens through file system
- Single-writer principle prevents coordination conflicts
- Scheduling ensures proper execution order
- Dwight (research) runs first, others consume his output
## Implementation Timeline and Best Practices
- Don't try to build everything in one weekend
- Start with basic files, add complexity gradually over 4 weeks
- Files evolve and become more precise over time
- Success comes from daily interaction and feedback, not prompt engineering

sources.raw_excerpt:
## Introduction to Tokenization
- Tokenization converts text into sequences of tokens (words, subwords, or characters)
- Tokens are assigned unique integers that map to vector representations
- Essential preprocessing step before LLM training and inference
- Example: 'Hello, world!' becomes ['Hello', ',', 'world', '!']
## Why Tokenization is Necessary
- Language models process numbers, not raw text
- Converts 10GB+ text datasets into numerical format
- Workflow: text → tokens → integers → vectors → model processing → integers → text
- Without tokenization, LLMs cannot operate on textual data
## Naive Tokenization Approaches
- Character-level: Small vocab but very long sequences, poor language diversity
- Word-level: Large vocab size, high computational complexity, still language-limited
- Both approaches have significant drawbacks for multilingual LLM training
- Need for subword tokenization as middle ground solution
## Unicode and UTF-8 Encoding Challenges
- Unicode ord function: Very large vocab size, unstable due to changing standards
- UTF-8 encoding: Limited to 256 vocab size, creates very long sequences
- UTF-8 uses 1-4 bytes per character, better ASCII compatibility
- Need compression method to increase vocab while reducing sequence length
## Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) Algorithm
- Iteratively replaces most common character pairs with unused placeholder bytes
- Example: 'aaabdaaabac' → 'ZabdZabac' (Z=aa) → 'ZYdZYac' (Y=ab) → 'XdXac' (X=ZY)
- Compression stops when no pairs occur more than once
- Decompression reverses the replacement process using lookup table
## Custom Tokenizer Implementation
- 5-step process: UTF-8 encode → convert to integers → find frequent pairs → replace with new tokens → repeat
- Vocabulary starts at 256 (0-255), expands with merges
- Encode function: text → UTF-8 bytes → merge pairs → return token list
- Decode function: token IDs → concatenate vocab bytes → UTF-8 decode → return text
- Training on multiple languages improves tokenizer performance

## Message Roles and Instructions
- Instructions parameter: high-level behavior guidance, takes priority
- Three message roles: developer (highest priority), user, assistant
- Developer messages: system rules and business logic
- User messages: inputs and configuration
- Instructions only apply to current request, not conversation history
## Reusable Prompts and Templates
- Create reusable prompts in OpenAI dashboard with placeholders

- Use Markdown headers/lists for structure and hierarchy
- XML tags to delineate content boundaries and add metadata
- Developer message structure: Identity → Instructions → Examples → Context
- Position frequently reused content early for prompt caching benefits
## Advanced Techniques
- Few-shot learning: include input/output examples to steer model behavior
- Show diverse range of examples for pattern recognition
- Include relevant context for proprietary data or constraints
- RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) for external data integration
- Context window planning: models have token limits (100k to 1M tokens)
## Model-Specific Prompting Strategies
- GPT models (gpt-5): need precise, explicit instructions with logic and data
- Reasoning models: work better with high-level guidance, like senior coworker

## Introduction and Importance of Prompt Evaluation
- Prompt evaluation is becoming a core engineering discipline for AI teams
- Teams are moving from intuitive to standardized, measurable evaluation
- Structured frameworks ensure quality, safety, cost and latency targets
- Small prompt changes can materially impact output faithfulness, tone, and latency
## Core Evaluation Dimensions
- Quality: Correctness, faithfulness, relevance, helpfulness, safety, tone alignment
- Consistency: Stability across datasets, versions, and models with variance analysis
- Cost: Token usage, latency, runtime overhead across prompts and models
- Rubric-based evaluators provide explainable scores for auditability
## Four-Layer Evaluation Framework
- Offline prompt tests: Bulk comparisons using curated datasets
- Multi-turn simulation: Dynamic agent workflows with tool-call evaluation
- Online monitoring: Production traces for quality checks and drift detection
- Human-in-the-loop: Domain expertise for safety and brand tone calibration
## Dataset Design and Coverage
- Task diversity: Varying complexity, ambiguity, domain specificity
- Scenario realism: Multi-turn conversations with clarifications and tool calls
- Edge cases: Adversarial instructions, prompt injection, safety-sensitive content
- Voice agents require varied audio quality and accent coverage
## Evaluator Design Approaches
- Programmatic checks: Schema conformity, tool-call structure validation
- Statistical metrics: Latency distributions, token usage, cost per request
- LLM-as-a-Judge: Rubric-based scoring for clarity, coherence, faithfulness
- Calibration against human annotations ensures fairness and reproducibility
## A/B Testing and Experimentation
- Clear objectives: Higher faithfulness, lower latency, reduced hallucinations
- Randomized assignment with unbiased traffic distribution
- Metric bundles combining quality, consistency, and cost
- Statistical rigor with sufficient samples for confidence-based decisions
## Operationalization and Deployment
- Version control linking evaluation outcomes to prompt metadata
- Progressive rollout with rule-based variables and canary testing
- Reproducible retrieval using SDK filtering by tags
- Automated rollback protocols for regression detection
## Agent and RAG-Specific Evaluation
- Tool-call evaluation: Selection, argument correctness, schema conformity
- RAG evaluation: Precision, recall, relevance, faithfulness to sources
- Trajectory analysis: Multi-turn coherence, task completion, error handling
- Trace analysis for pinpointing exact reasoning failures
## Production Monitoring and Best Practices
- Distributed tracing for prompt execution, tool calls, retrieval steps
- Automated checks on live logs for drift detection
- Data curation converting live traces to evaluation datasets
- Common pitfalls: insufficient samples, overfitting, ignoring multi-turn dynamics

sources.raw_excerpt:
## What are Embeddings and Why They Matter
- Embeddings convert discrete, high-dimensional data into continuous, low-dimensional vectors
- Semantically similar items are placed closer together in vector space
- Example: 'cat' and 'dog' words represented as nearby 300-dimensional vectors
- Essential for NLP, recommendation systems, and categorical feature handling
## How Embeddings are Created and Trained
- Neural networks learn embeddings by minimizing loss functions during training
- Word2Vec example: predicting target words from context
- Movie recommendation example: users and movies get embeddings based on interactions
- Dimensionality is a hyperparameter (50, 100, 300) affecting detail vs computational cost
- Embeddings start random and improve through backpropagation
## Practical Implementation of Embeddings
- Implemented as lookup tables/matrices in PyTorch and TensorFlow
- Example: 10,000 vocabulary with 300-dim embeddings = 10,000x300 matrix
- Input ID retrieves corresponding row as vector representation
- Must handle out-of-vocabulary items via hashing or default vectors
- Choice between pre-trained embeddings (GloVe) vs training from scratch
- More efficient than one-hot encoding for large vocabularies

sources.title: Evolution of Word Embeddings: From Word2Vec to Context-Aware Models
sources.summary: Word2Vec revolutionized NLP by converting words to dense numerical vectors that capture semantic similarity. CBoW predicts target words from context, while Skip-gram predicts context from target words. GloVe improves on Word2Vec by leveraging global co-occurrence statistics across the entire corpus
key_takeaways: Word2Vec revolutionized NLP by converting words to dense numerical vectors that capture semantic similarity
CBoW predicts target words from context, while Skip-gram predicts context from target words
GloVe improves on Word2Vec by leveraging global co-occurrence statistics across the entire corpus
FastText handles morphologically rich languages by using subword character n-grams
All these methods lack context awareness - same word gets same vector regardless of meaning in different contexts
Modern transformer-based models like BERT address context limitations but Word2Vec remains foundational
[Action] Use Word2Vec for basic semantic similarity tasks and as baseline embeddings
[Action] Choose GloVe when global document-level relationships are important
[Action] Apply FastText for morphologically rich languages or out-of-vocabulary words
[Action] Consider context-aware models (BERT, GPT) for tasks requiring nuanced word meaning understanding
[Action] Evaluate embedding quality using word similarity and analogy tasks
[Action] Leverage pretrained embeddings to bootstrap NLP applications
sources.raw_excerpt:
## Introduction to Word Embeddings
- Computers need numerical representations to understand words
- Embeddings convert words to mathematical vectors where similar meanings have similar values
- Early n-gram vectors were large, sparse, and suffered from curse of dimensionality
- Neural probabilistic language model (2003) introduced continuous dense vectors
## Word2Vec Architecture Overview
- Word2Vec framework emerged in 2013
- Two primary architectures: Continuous Bag of Words (CBoW) and Skip-gram
- Uses context windows to learn word relationships
- Transforms words into dense vector representations
## Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBoW) Model
- Predicts target word from surrounding context words
- Uses context window (e.g., size 2) to select input words
- Converts words to one-hot vectors, projects to lower dimension, then back to vocabulary size
- Training involves sliding context window across sentences
## Skip-gram Model
- Reverse of CBoW: predicts context words from target word
- Input is single word, outputs are surrounding context words
- Creates multiple training pairs from each context window
- Name reflects 'skipping' over words to predict context
## Word2Vec Advantages and Limitations
- Pros: Overcomes curse of dimensionality, semantic similarity, pretrained embeddings available, self-supervised
- Cons: Limited global information preservation, struggles with morphologically rich languages, lacks broad context awareness
- Local context focus may miss long-range dependencies
## GloVe: Global Vectors
- Addresses Word2Vec's limitation of not using global context
- Uses word-word co-occurrence matrix across entire corpus
- Works with ratios of co-occurrence probabilities rather than raw probabilities
- Example: P(k|ice)/P(k|steam) ratio determines word association with ice vs steam
## FastText for Morphologically Rich Languages
- Addresses Word2Vec's limitation with morphologically rich languages
- Example: Kannada word 'house' has different forms (mane, maneyalli, maneyinda)
- Uses subword information: breaks words into character n-grams (3-6 grams)
- Aggregates n-gram vectors to represent entire words
- Benefits Arabic, Turkish, Finnish, and Indian languages
## Context Awareness Limitations
- Word2Vec generates same vector regardless of context
- Example: 'queen' in 'drag queen slays' vs 'ace and queen' has same vector
- Advanced architectures like LSTM and transformers (BERT, GPT) address this
- Modern models generate context-sensitive word representations

sources.raw_excerpt:
## Introduction to Vector Embeddings
- Vector embeddings are essential for AI applications like RAG, agents, NLP, semantic search, and image search
- An embedding is a compact representation of raw data transformed into a vector of floating-point numbers
- High-dimensional data is mapped into lower-dimensional space while preserving semantic relationships
- Semantically similar items are placed closer together in vector space
## Types of Vector Embeddings
- Word embeddings: Most common type for NLP tasks like translation and sentiment analysis
- Sentence embeddings: Capture semantic meaning for chatbots and machine translation
- Document embeddings: Represent entire documents for recommendation systems and classification
- Image embeddings: Used for image recognition and content-based recommendations
- Audio embeddings: Capture rhythm, tone, pitch for voice recognition and music recommendations
- Graph embeddings: Represent nodes and edges for link prediction and community detection
## How Neural Networks Create Embeddings
- Neural networks create embeddings through representation learning
- Process involves mapping high-dimensional data to lower-dimensional spaces
- Embedding layers have randomly initialized weights that are refined through backpropagation
- Training task shapes the learned embeddings to capture semantic relationships
- Example: Movie review sentiment classification adjusts word embeddings based on context
## How Vector Embeddings Work
- Embeddings represent objects as points in multidimensional vector space
- Distance metrics quantify relationships: Euclidean distance, cosine similarity, Manhattan distance
- Cosine similarity measures angle between vectors regardless of magnitude
- Example: 'cat' [1.2, 0.8] and 'dog' [1.0, 0.9] are closer than 'car' [0.3, -1.5]
## Creating Vector Embeddings
- Vectorization process: collect data, preprocess, chunk, embed
- Popular text embedding models: OpenAI text-embedding-3, Google Gemini, Cohere Embed
- Image embeddings: CNNs like VGG, Inception, or OpenAI CLIP
- pgvectorizer for PostgreSQL keeps embeddings and relational data in sync
- Code example using OpenAI's text-embedding API with Python
## Storing Vector Embeddings
- Traditional databases insufficient for vector data complexity
- Vector databases specialized for storing and retrieving vector embeddings
- PostgreSQL with pgvector extension enables vector storage alongside relational data
- Combines vector data with business data, metrics, and analytics
- Enables applications like RAG, NLP, computer vision, and semantic search

- 4. Optimize retrieval depth in RAG pipelines
- 5. Apply quantization and distillation strategically

- 7. Instrument cost at feature and workflow level
- 8. Establish continuous AI FinOps practices
## Implementation Best Practices

- Standardize prompt templates and output schemas

- Track cost per feature for better product decisions
